docs.title: Basic Mathematics — Student Problem Notebook
docs.text:
The lecture PDF is required reading

This repository and AI tools do not replace the lecture material. Before beginning repository work, read the complete introduction to the Basic Mathematics PDF. It explains the purpose, philosophy, methodology, expectations, role of lectures and exercise classes, mathematical-note standard, feedback process, and appropriate use of AI. Continue reading the corresponding PDF chapters throughout the course. Using only the problem templates without reading the lecture notes defeats the purpose of the course.

Start here

If you do not know what to do, open or upload START_HERE_WITH_AI.md. It is the single entry point for both the student and an AI assistant.

This site is a structured workspace for the twelve weekly problem sets accompanying the Basic Mathematics lecture notes.

The objective is not to collect answers. The objective is to learn how to read mathematical notation, construct justified arguments, prepare professional notes, present solutions, receive feedback, and improve earlier work.

docs.title: AI and GitHub Workflow
docs.text: AI is a learning assistant, not the author of work that the student does not understand. Free AI tools are sufficient for the support expected in this course: explaining notation, generating simpler examples, checking reasoning, identifying missing steps, and improving mathematical writing.

docs.title: Source PDF
docs.text:
The authoritative Basic Mathematics lecture PDF is available here:

  • View the PDF on GitHub
  • Download the PDF directly

Before starting a weekly problem set, read the corresponding PDF chapter. For the exact current problem statement, follow the week-to-LaTeX mapping in Source Material. Use the matching problem_XX.md file as the workspace for the solution.

docs.title: Work on one problem at a time
docs.text:
Use one focused conversation for one problem. Do not paste the complete problem set into one conversation and request all answers at once. A focused context makes it easier to question every step and produce a substantive note.

Repeat this process for all twenty assigned problems.

docs.title: Recommended conversation
docs.text:
Begin with:

Read SOURCE_MATERIAL.md, NOTE_GUIDELINES.md, AI_WORKFLOW.md, the corresponding PDF chapter, the selected problem in the mapped LaTeX source file, and the matching problem file. Help me understand the problem first. Do not edit the file until I confirm that I understand the method.

Ask questions such as:

  • What mathematical object does each symbol represent?
  • What is given and what must be found?
  • Why does this method apply?
  • Which definition or theorem is being used?
  • Why is this transition valid?
  • How can the result be checked?
  • What should I be able to explain during class?

After understanding the solution:

Update only the selected problem_XX.md file. Copy that problem's statement faithfully from the mapped LaTeX source file, then complete every required section. Connect formulas with explanatory sentences. Write display formulas as $$formula$$ without spaces or blank lines inside the delimiters. Do not edit other problems.

Before committing:

Review the note for missing assumptions, undefined notation, unjustified transitions, and calculation errors. Keep it concise but complete.

After reading the result yourself:

Commit the note with a message such as Add justified solution for Week 3, Problem 4.

docs.title: Commit as the result of learning
docs.text:
The raw conversation does not need to be committed. Its carefully reviewed result becomes the repository artifact:

  1. conversation and questions,
  2. understanding,
  3. written note,
  4. student verification,
  5. commit,
  6. presentation,
  7. feedback,
  8. revision and another commit.

The commit history should show systematic work and improvement throughout the semester.

docs.title: 1. Fork the repository
docs.text: Create a public fork of dchorazkiewicz/Math_Problems_Repo on your own GitHub account. All of your notes and commits belong in your fork, not in the instructor's source repository.

docs.title: 2. Connect your fork to AI
docs.text: If your AI tool supports a GitHub connection, authorise access to your own fork. The exact available actions depend on the tool and its permissions.

docs.title: 3. Read the course rules
docs.text:
Before solving problems, read:

  • NOTE_GUIDELINES.md,
  • AI_WORKFLOW.md,
  • SOURCE_MATERIAL.md,
  • the selected problem_XX.md file.

Students read the Basic Mathematics PDF. AI assistants locate the exact current statements in the mapped LaTeX files listed under Source Material, not in the empty repository templates. Copy only the statement of the problem you are beginning into its matching file.

docs.title: 4. Work systematically
docs.text: Each week contains twenty problems. Work on one problem at a time, but complete the entire assigned set. Commit reviewed notes regularly rather than uploading everything shortly before class.

docs.title: 5. Present and revise
docs.text: During exercise classes, be ready to display and explain any prepared solution. Feedback given to one student is useful to the whole group. Improve incomplete notes in later commits.

docs.title: Assessment
docs.text: The exercise component evaluates systematic preparation, complete problem sets, mathematical justification, presentation, use of feedback, and development over time. The final examination is assessed separately and verifies independent mathematical knowledge without AI support.

docs.title: One problem, one complete note
docs.text:
Prepare a separate, self-contained note for every assigned problem. Students read the problem in the lecture PDF; AI assistants use the corresponding current LaTeX file mapped under Source Material. When beginning a note, copy the complete statement of that one problem into its Problem statement section so that the finished note remains understandable without reopening the source material.

Students are expected to prepare the complete assigned problem set. One polished solution does not replace the remaining problems, and many bare answers do not replace complete reasoning.

docs.title: Required structure
docs.text:
Every note must contain:

  1. Problem statement — copied faithfully from the current course source when work on that problem begins.
  2. Given information and goal — what is known and what must be found, proved, or explained.
  3. Method — the definitions, theorems, or techniques selected and why they apply.
  4. Solution and justification — formulas connected by sentences explaining important transitions.
  5. Verification — checks of calculations, assumptions, domains, dimensions, or interpretation when appropriate.
  6. Conclusion — a direct answer to the original question.

docs.title: Mathematical writing
docs.text:
Formulas do not explain themselves. Use complete sentences to show why an operation is performed, which result is being used, and how one step leads to the next.

It is not necessary to describe every elementary arithmetic operation. Explain every mathematically important transition.

Write as briefly as possible, but as completely as necessary.

docs.title: Incomplete work
docs.text:
The following will be treated as incomplete and returned for revision:

  • a bare final answer,
  • an unexplained sequence of formulas,
  • undefined notation,
  • missing assumptions or unjustified transitions,
  • an AI-generated note that the student cannot read, explain, or present.

The student is responsible for every sentence and formula committed to the repository.

docs.title: Display mathematics
docs.text:
Write a display formula without spaces or blank lines after the opening delimiter or before the closing delimiter:

$$formula$$

Do not use a spaced multiline form of the double-dollar delimiter.

docs.title: Start Here with AI
docs.text:
Read the PDF introduction first

The repository and AI do not replace the lecture notes. The complete introduction to the Basic Mathematics PDF is required reading before beginning, and the corresponding chapters must be read throughout the course.

Download or share the single onboarding document:

Open START_HERE_WITH_AI.md

It explains the purpose of the course, the roles of lectures and exercise classes, what to do after a lecture, where the PDF and problem sources are, how notes and AI should be used, what is assessed, and where to find detailed instructions.

You can upload that Markdown file to an AI chat and ask:

Read this course-orientation document and use its links when I ask for details. Explain what the lectures and exercise classes require, where the materials are, and how I should prepare my work. Do not guess the current teaching week or timetable.

  5. Prepare the complete set of twenty problems.
  6. Commit reviewed work regularly.
